title: Scottish tablet
subtitle: Scottish sugar confection beaten to a dense, crumbly, fudge-like texture
excerpt: Scottish tablet is a firm, dense, intensely sweet confection made from sugar, butter, condensed milk, and whole milk cooked to the soft-ball stage and then beaten vigorously off the heat until the mixture grains and becomes opaque. The result is harder and more granular than English fudge, crumbling at the edges when snapped but melting quickly on the tongue. It is extraordinarily sweet and very rich. A Scottish tradition at fetes, school fairs, and Christmas.
sourceNotes: Scottish tablet is a traditional Scottish confection documented in Scottish household cookery literature from the eighteenth century. F Marian McNeill, The Scots Kitchen (1929), gives a recipe for tablet consistent with the modern form made from sugar, butter, condensed milk, and whole milk. The beating of the mixture off the heat to cause crystallisation and a crumbly, dry texture is described in Scottish confectionery literature of the nineteenth century as the defining technique.

content.text: The critical moment is recognising when

: it turns from a glossy liquid to a thick, matte, grainy mass. At that point pour immediately. Keep a wooden spoon in a jug of cold water to cool your hand; the mixture is extremely hot during beating.

Butter the square tin. Combine the sugar, condensed milk, butter, and whole milk in a large heavy saucepan. Stir over medium heat until the sugar dissolves. Bring to the boil, clip on the sugar thermometer, and cook over medium heat, stirring frequently to prevent scorching on the base, until the mixture reaches 118°C. This takes about 20 to 25 minutes. The mixture will be thick and pale caramel-coloured.

Remove from the heat. Add the vanilla. Beat the mixture vigorously with a wooden spoon or hand-held electric whisk for 3 to 5 minutes until it loses its shine, thickens, and becomes matte and opaque. Work quickly: as soon as it looks grainy and pourable, tip it into the tin at once and spread level with the back of the spoon. Leave to cool at room temperature for at least 1 hour. Mark into squares while still slightly warm, then leave to set completely before cutting through fully. Store in an airtight tin.
